title: 2025/11/12 - SSC (Part 2)
displayTitle: 2025-11-12 - Social Services and Community Committee - Briefing on the Integrated Data Infrastructure [Part 2]
topic: Briefing on the Integrated Data Infrastructure
description: Wednesday, 12 November 2025 | Social Services and Community Committee Briefing on the Integrated Data Infrastructure Statistics New Zealand Rachael Milicich, Deputy Government Statistician and Deputy Chief Executive for Insights & Statistics Simon Ross, General Manager, Data Operations and Infrastructure
descriptionSummary: Briefing on the Integrated Data Infrastructure. Statistics New Zealand. Rachael Milicich, Deputy Government Statistician and Deputy Chief Executive for Insights & Statistics

Yeah, Sorry.

Well, good afternoon and welcome to the Social Services CUNY Select Committee. Um, thank you very much for coming in. Um, so gonna give us a briefing this afternoon on the integrated data infrastructure, which I'm paying for sort of more, more understanding and awareness of our parliamentarians and probably others as well around this. But look, um, I'll hand it to you to introduce yourselves and to give an overview, um, into x to take, I think taking the production that I think may not be a wide setting, uh, from some

of the, you know, people at the table and, and, and across New Zealand, actually. Uh, so you can give us an oversight and then it's, and then providing how much time I have, you know, questions for members would be great. No problem. Oh, well, thank you for the opportunity to, um, come and speak about, uh, the value of, uh, integrated data today. Uh, my name is Rachel.

I'm one of the deputies, um, at Steps nz and with me is, uh, Simon Ross, who's our general manager for data operations and Infrastructure. So we're going to do a little bit of a TAG team, if that's okay for you. So, uh, I'll talk just very briefly about, um, the role of stats in this context, and then I'll hand to Simon and he'll talk more about the integrated data infrastructure itself.

Thank you. So, uh, STAT is hopefully many of you know, uh, stats NZ collects information, um, uh, about, uh, people in organizations. Um, and we use that data to provide insights, uh, statistics and data about New Zealand. Uh, we source a wide range of data that includes administrative data, like, uh, tax, uh, private sector data from data intermediaries, as well as, um, from surveying, uh, the government statistician at Stats NZ holds, uh, the government, uh, chief data steward role.

Uh, this role supports the use of data as a resource across government to help deliver better services, uh, to New Zealanders within the research landscape. Stats N Z's role is to ingest and link the data, provide the infrastructure, and ensure that the data is kept safe through the safeguards that surround the IDI.

Within the data system, uh, government agencies, tertiary sector and research institutions set sector priorities, um, conduct the research and implement the findings of the research into, uh, policy direction, uh, or service evaluation, um, or improvements.

Stats NZ responds to the needs of the research community by adding or modifying, uh, new data to the IDI and that enables or supports the research stats. NZI also provides supports in terms of access, uh, to use the IDI stats. N Z's key piece of legislation is the Data and Statistics Act, uh, 2022.

The key parts of this legislation include requirements that researchers in the public interest and is, uh, carried out by appropriate researchers only there are strong confidentiality requirements, so people can be assured their data is safe and protected. Uh, for example, data cannot be released where it might, uh, be able to identify an individual.

The government statistician must also publish information about research access, and researchers must must publish their results. So New Zealanders can see the way their data is managed and used. Uh, the independence of the government statistician in this process is a unique point of difference. Um, and service delivery agencies, um, uh, could be conflicted in their hosting of the data against their service delivery mandate, whereas Stats NZ can play a more neutral role in this, uh, context. So I'll hand to Simon.

Uh, thank you Rachel Koto. Um, so I'm Simon Ross, and just looking to take the opportunity to reinforce a few of the key points about the IDI from the information that we shared in advance. So the IDI is the government's most comprehensive research database. It contains de-identified data about people and households, contains a range of data, um, about events and services such as from education, income, benefits, migration, justice and health.

Um, and it's built from data supplied by government agencies from stats NZ surveys and from non-government organizations. The RDI I is built around a central linking spine. It's a list that aims to estimate every, the ever resident population of New Zealand. So every person who's ever been here is a resident, and it's made by combining tax, birth and migration records.

Making the data accessible while keeping it safe is an overriding priority for stats NZ in its stewardship of the IDI. So we use the five safes and the HARRY frameworks to manage safe access to the data. The IDI is a critical tool for governments and the wider data system with many agencies now relying on the IDI to inform service planning and delivery, as well as program evaluation.

The IDI is also used by, used widely beyond governments, including for research within the academic and tertiary sectors, um, for looking at outcomes of key population groups and the factors that affect these outcomes. The IDI is used by research consultancies that carry out research on behalf of others to support them in accessing the insights they need.

And I guess just to a little more on that, um, if you're a agency or a community group and you might not have the technical expertise to gain insights from the IDI, um, it's useful that these kind of experienced, uh, consultancies are able to be contracted to provide that expertise and those insights, just in terms of what the IEI can and can't do, it can link data from multiple sources over time, which allows cohorts to be understood across their lifespan, can help identify characteristics of groups with positive and negative outcomes, and can evaluate

how effective service and interventions are, such as the effectiveness of alternative education services or teen parent units. And the effectiveness of the Housing First program, which is a study done by the University of Otago, it can also be used to tailor interventions to people based on characteristics they share with groups that have been studied. In terms of things the IDI cannot do, um, it can't follow individuals who are using services.

So it's not a case management system. It can't identify specific individuals who are at risk or would benefit from a specific intervention, and it can't identify specific individuals who are abusing systems or take enforcement action. So the focus of the analysis is at that group level, not that individual level. Uh, just in terms of final comments, um, some key pieces of research that might be relevant to this committee.

Uh, so we've had a study on healthcare accessibility and aging in white Taha Canterbury. So the research explores how residential mobility affects healthcare for older adults in Canterbury in the West Coast. The study will assess where the current healthcare infrastructure meets the needs of an aging and increasingly diverse population study of child and adolescent development in New Zealand.

This report aims to consolidate data on child development and identify areas for early intervention and a study on neurodegenerative diseases in former rugby players. So this study examines health impacts of playing rugby at various levels, focusing on neurodegenerative diseases and former first class male players also high highlights the innovative uses of big data in sports health research.

And just one final comment. Um, stats is not expert in the, you know, these individual pieces of research that are done in the IDI. We need to know enough to evaluate potential projects, uh, but we do not have the full details of findings of research projects. Um, we do seek, however, to ensure that these are published and publicized for transparency, and so that their value can be realized. Thank you.

Great, thank you very much. Might kick it off with a questions. Um, is there, is there anything equivalent to this in any other jurisdiction globally? Or is this reasonably unique? Uh, I would say New Zealand is recognized as a leader in, you know, what we've been able to do with bringing the amount of data together that we have.

There are various other jurisdictions that have examples of this. Um, so there are some examples in Australia, there's a Western Australia data linkage infrastructure. The, um, the Australian Bureau of Statistics also has a data linkage program. There are a couple of examples in Canada, one in British Columbia and one in Ontario.

Uh, it's been worked on in the UK as well. I would say we are more advanced than, um, than England in this space. But, um, Scotland has an example in the health space, which is quite advanced. So there are a number of examples around the world, but they're not all as comprehensive as the one that we've been able to build in New Zealand. And, um, the, you, you need to strike a balance clearly between, um, data integration and, and dashboarding that data and also concerns around privacy and data sovereignty.

How do you balance that? Uh, so we are really focused on making sure that the data we bring in is safe, and we have a number of processes to make sure that is, so that is begins in talking with the people, um, who hold the data in the first place to make sure that they have the rights, uh, permissions and sort of privacy statements and things that would allow the data to be on supplied to us.

We've also got the protections of the Data and Stats Act, which are important there. So that covers whether we can bring it in the first place and all our obligations once we hold it. Uh, once we've decided that we can bring it in and we've done privacy and fact assessments and we've done data sharing agreements that cover that, um, we then have the protections, and this is all under the five safes framework where every piece of research that wants to come in and access that data set is being scrutinized to make sure it's in the public interest.

And so the need ticking a pie Harry framework is more bringing a kind of ethics perspective to it and an indigenous data perspective. So I think you'll find there are controls in various areas and they overlap and they work together to make sure that both it's safe to bring the data in and then it's safe for whatever projects are seeking to use it. One of the, one of the things I've seen over the, over the years is, uh, the challenge in, I guess you might say, um, data dashboarding.

So decision makers can actually understand what's going on and what, what are the drivers, what are the population movements, et cetera, and what that means for policy decisions they need to make both at a local government level and a central government level. Um, how, what work has been done to dashboard that decision makers across, you know, so both local and central government?

Well, I guess we are not the ones doing that work, so we are sort of providing the infrastructure and making sure it's available to approved projects. There are multiple different government departments who are using that data for their various purposes. So, uh, for instance, um, health as a project. And so they'll do health research and they'll link it through to social factors and they will, um, present that information for their constituencies.

The social investment agency has quite a significant dashboarding program where they're bringing data out of the IDI and they're making it available publicly. Um, and a lot of that is broken down at a regional level, so it's quite valuable, uh, throughout the country from that perspective. Um, anything else you'd wanna comment on? Yeah, Well, in terms of our own, uh, statistics and making them available, uh, we use outputs from, um, the IDI in that process as well.

What, sorry, could you say that again? Um, in terms of our own statistical releases and programs, um, we also use, um, outputs from the IDI in order to underpin, uh, that work as well. So, uh, in terms of dashboarding, um, it's really those data visualizations that we have available, um, on our website as well.

So do do local government, um, access this or use it at all to your knowledge? So I don't have any examples with me of projects that are specifically from local government agencies. So I think that's an area that we probably haven't explored a lot yet. Yeah. And what, what are, what are some opportunities, um, that haven't been sort of fleshed out yet that, that you, you may see on the, on the horizon that, uh, could be useful?

Well, I think there's a number of, um, areas where the government's got social investment priorities and are increasingly, you know, seeking to develop projects. So in those areas, um, working with NGOs to bring in new data and then looking to connect that NGO data with the data that's already in the IDI and being able to provide sort of dashboards and things back to those NGOs, um, that's a real area of growth and we'd expect, you know, quite a lot of that to happen over time.

Um, NGO data is a current, there isn't a lot of NGO data in the IDI at the moment. And so it's an area where, um, working with the government and the social investment agency we are looking to grow. Its over time. Um, I think, you know, there's 370 or so active projects in the I to I at the moment.

So, you know, lots of people, uh, have over the years, you know, seen value in the data sets that we've got online and sort of put together a project and brought it to us, and we've evaluated that and now they're working away in it. So, um, there are many projects that are going on right now. Um, and so as I say, we scrutinize the projects on their way in and we are monitoring that they're doing the right things, but we are not, um, monitoring the content of those projects and what comes out of them.

Um, so it's really our job to make that data available and to make sure people are using it safely and then they take it and they, uh, develop the insights and then publish it. Okay. And I, I mean, there's two, I'll go go to other questions shortly, but, um, I guess there's two two co cohorts that immediately come to mind for me. Um, one, one is, um, young people and especially those with, with various complexities that that may make it difficult.

And so really interested in what that data can be used for and that, so to ensure we get best service delivery to them. The other, the other is our aging population, which is quite a significant bulge, uh, coming from New Zealand. And, and again, that, that data set and what we can, you know, how that can be used actually better informed decision making going forward.

It is sort of a very broad question, but I, I, I raised some comments on the usefulness of the data set for, for those two Co Yeah, I can. So, um, just in the older people, there is a project on expenditure patterns of retiree households. So this study analyzes household wealth trends across demographics and explores how wealth affects life outcomes and informs debates on retirement policy taxation, uh, and older age poverty.

Um, just trying to see if we've got an example, a youth example in here. We do. Uh, so one example, um, this youth example, so it has been identified that, uh, driver licensing or lack of driver licensing in rural communities for young people was affecting justice outcomes.

And so identified that rural males, many of them were entering the justice system after being caught driving without a license. And this sort of led to vines and enforcements and things that could have been averted had there been, um, you know, driver's licenses able to be had earlier. And so, uh, program was run to target driver licensing to support to try and break the cycle.

And so in terms of short term outcomes, after one to two years, more young men obtaining licenses, fewer driving offenses, reduced police interactions in the medium term around five years, improved employment and education outcomes, lower benefit reliance, especially in rural areas where licenses often essential for work. And in the longer term, over 10 years, fewer crashes, hospitalizations, and a CC claims fewer justice and corrections and directions and reduced pressure on public services.

So, I mean, the two examples I gave, there are only two examples. There are many things that people looked at for older people and for young people in the IEI. So it's, um, data that's available for people to bring projects forward, really. And I'm sorry, I'll get this, I'll get this, got a couple. Um, I just thought that we're gonna Bribe you with this batch, Uh, that, uh, the idea has, has grown since 1991, or see, was it seven, um, to comprise over 500 tables containing 15 billion plus rows of data.

Now we're obviously entering an era of artificial intelligence. We've got an ability to analyze data in a way that's never been able to be done before, but also you need to ensure that you, that it stays within that data set. Mm-hmm. Yes. Um, what opportunities, uh, are available there that you sort of have seen you? Is it, is that still no one's really turned their mind to add?

So the, I is currently run on what we would describe as reasonably creaky infrastructure. Um, it is safe and secure, but it is not hugely advanced. And so I think that limits the ability to bring kind of modern and advanced AI tooling to bear. Yeah, I think there are also kind of important ethical questions about whether this, you know, rich resource of data about people, whether it's appropriate to bring those tools in there.

And so they are kind of questions that we haven't dealt with yet, but we would expect to work through a kind of ethical framework to do so. Um, we are working on a business case for improvements to the IDI for the future. And so, you know, if we were to advance the infrastructure, then those questions become really important. Like is it the right thing to do to bring in, um, AI tools?

But it's something that we haven't explicitly addressed yet. Okay. I've got now several up. For me, it's a different question. Um, but the first one is, um, I'll just pick up that issue about the ai and obviously there's the security issues that are involved in a, a developing technology, but there's already security issues.

But one of the benefits of this has gotta be the linkage with other countries, hasn't it? Because you can expand your pattern recognition and you can learn more. What do you do about making sure that your development is able to work, um, in a synergistic Yeah.

Way with other countries who are doing similar things so that you can power up the wisdom that you're getting out of this, of the information. Um, so I think there's, uh, probably a couple of points. So, uh, so we are part of, uh, a wide network, um, across the international statistical community.

Um, so some of the, uh, uh, studies and, um, organizations, um, Simon referenced earlier. So we are all developing, um, and evolving those things together. So we do share, uh, practice around, um, ethics confidentiality, but also obviously a mindful of, uh, things like the security settings.

So within, um, our context, um, we meet, uh, a lot of the government, um, requirements for, um, protection and security. Um, uh, yeah, we, we have to meet those, that's a really important part of, um, keeping, uh, the data safe. Uh, we also, uh, uh, regularly audit, um, uh, our practices as well to make sure that, uh, we are keeping things safe.

Um, it's very important in terms of, you know, the social license, the license to operate from New Zealanders that, you know, they have confidence in our ability to, um, source data col, um, collect it, uh, integrate it and, and keep that safe while still providing the benefits that I think we've talked about today.

Um, we don't, we haven't got joined up databases and network of databases across, uh, the world. Um, but obviously, um, the research that is made available is available to everybody. And so there are opportunities, um, obviously for researchers to link up internationally and work out, um, what types of studies might, uh, be valuable in that context as well.

So, um, so are the, is it, would it be fair to say that you're designing mindful of that kind of, um, ecosystem that's broader than just New Zealand? Absolutely. Okay. And can I ask you, this is a sort of philosophical question and you might just say it's beyond your scope.

Sure. But it occurs to me that what you are doing might mean that there's a huge boost in understanding of proactive, um, policy responses. So because you are studying those or you've got that information that we might see people finally understand that there is an ambulance at the bottom of the cliff and then there's proactive money into things so that they never happen.

Do you see that in what, in the way that the information's being used that we might see a, um, confirmation of proactive policies working? You just gave the example, I think of the one that was, um, the driver's license one is one of those, isn't it?

Um, But you are avoiding trouble. Well, Stats is not really involved Yeah. In evaluating, you know, all of the different pieces of research that are being done. So I think these insights are quite diffusely, um, perhaps found throughout government.

So, you know, different projects, find 'em in different places. Um, one thing we do observe though, is that sometimes people will go in and they'll be looking at a specific thing, specific set of research questions, but there'll be kind of incidental findings that come out of that that prove useful in some other areas. So that is definitely a value of bringing the data together, um, and allowing these analysis to go on.

So, um, there have been examples of interesting and useful findings that have not necessarily been the kind of core reason that that research was being done in the first place. Yeah. Yeah. Okay. Alright, Thank you. Um, just a quick question about the data.

So is the data that you make available sold, or is it provided as a service? Sorry, sold. Was that the first part of your question? Yes. Uh, so the data that we bring in, uh, we bring in with agreements with government agencies and others. And so we don't buy it nor do we sell it. We don't sell access to it.

Uh, so we, it's not our data, we don't own it. We host it on behalf of others and we make it available. Um, and people need to meet certain standards to be able to access. So it's provided as a service? Yes. It's provided as a service Yes. Under our appropriation. Right? Sure. Um, yeah, I think you mentioned someone at the beginning that you capture data from people who enter New Zealand, come to New Zealand. Was it?

Uh, so migration is one of the key data sets. Uh, so when we're trying to, um, define that ever resident population, um, we've got people who are born here and people who die here. So got those records. We've got people who work here through tax records. And then for people who come into the country, we're trying to capture their residency via the crossing of the border.

Okay. So it's not just international travelers or are you measuring that As No, so in fact, we're not really trying to capture people who might have just come here for two weeks as tourists. Like that's not the focus of it. But we do wanna capture people who move to New Zealand. So that's the value of those migration records, being able to figure out who those people are and make sure that they're in that core us. Uh, um, so just thinking about that from a tourism point of view, capturing people who are entering, even if it is temporarily, um, would seem to me

to be a a, a valuable measure to have or is that easily captured in other data or through through the entry Customs? Yeah, so we, yeah, we provide, um, statistics about, um, migration flows, people who are obviously coming to New Zealand, um, and leaving. And also tourism flows as well. Travelers. Yeah.

Okay. Great. Yeah, thank you Ola. Thank you. Um, for cheers. Um, I have a supplementary with Alice's question and it is more around how robust is the framework, how often do you audit it and um, around safeguards we may have already me, you this Yeah. Um, and the compliance around it.

So is our legislation fit for purpose or does it have to be amended over time? Because given the cyber all over the world, you know, from a New Zealand, New Zealand Inc. Perspective. Interesting. And I just, I'm really interested in the robust framework, robustness of the framework and, uh, legislation.

Uh, perhaps if we start with the legislation and then move on to the other frameworks. So we have the Data and Stats Act, which was updated in 2022, so that's a pretty recent piece of legislation. I think we think it's working pretty well and offers sort of a good balance of access to data and protection of data.

So I think we're pretty happy with that. Uh, we've also had our Privacy Act updated in 2020, so that's a relevant piece of legislation here. All the privacy impact assessments we do are done under that framework. Uh, so again, it's not, you know, not 2025, but it work was done on that fairly recently, Fairly recent. Um, in terms of the five safes frameworks, so some my notes on it, but, um, safe people, safe projects, safe settings, safe data, Safe outputs, Safe outputs.

Thank you Rachel. Um, so that framework is something that we've had in place for quite a long period of time, but exactly what we do under that framework, um, you know, we're always kinda looking to tighten up, um, and make sure that we, uh, applying it as best we can, uh, when it comes to projects that are going on in the IDI and then also the data labs themselves where people are accessing the data.

So we audit those data labs once a year to make sure that the physical security is right and also that the IT security is right. And then we have scrutiny of the projects both before they're approved to make sure that they're set up in the right way. And also as they go on, uh, researchers come on and off them, but we need to make sure that they are doing the things that they've said, uh, in the applications of those projects, not going off piece and doing other things with the data.

So I think all of those things coming together, like we have a good level of scrutiny, uh, on the sort of cyber security side of things. So I think the IDI sits within STAT mz, which needs to have cybersecurity across all of everything our operations. And so we are applying that at the edge of the organization. And then, so the IDI benefits from that.

Um, but it's the type of thing where that security, you know, always a focus trying to make sure that it is up to scratch. 'cause you need to do more over time rather than less. Yeah. Thank you. And just a really tiny set around threats and risks in the cyber space, and I'm sort of more looking overseas rather than here. 'cause it sounds like you've got a really robust framework. How sort of, are there, I mean, how does that work?

Do you get a like flag or how are you advised or? So neither of us are sort of chief technology officer or kind of our security people. So we have people in the organization who are responsible for monitoring those threats, I guess I would say. And yeah. Yeah, it's at all of organization level. Yeah.

And we can certainly provide the committee with, um, more information about how we do, uh, go about those things. Um, so more than happy to do That. It in here that the data labs aren't connected to the internet though. No. So that's gotta be a big help. Yeah, that's an important thing. So, you know, sequestered off, it's where you can access this data specifically and you can't access all that data that's talent needs from the same place.

Um, which I've just given myself a sub segue into a sub. Um, so because they're not connected to the data, labs aren't connected to the internet, how do you physically get the information out of there to, to customers or people who are requesting the information? Uh, well I guess there are connections from the data labs to the databases themselves, so people can do the research.

Uh, and so we have what we call output checking. So researchers are required to make the data confidentially, um, in order for it to be outputted. And so that's their responsibility as researchers. Uh, we then have a team in stats who does output checking. So they go in and they look at the outputs the researchers have created, and then make sure that all of our rules are being applied and no one could be identified from that data if it was to come out of the lab.

And then it's our team who takes the data outta the lab and makes it available to the researchers outside the lab. So that's how that process works. So how do you get it out if it's not connected to the, Uh, so within our infrastructure in stats nz, they're able to take those files and then make those available to researchers.

So, um, they are, I believe they're emailed to researchers once our staff take them out of the data Lab. So the co the they create a copy that's removed. Yeah, that's right. Yeah. Yeah. Cool. Um, thanks Mr. Chair. Thank you for coming in today. Um, I'm keen to know a little bit more about, um, tikanga Maori principles being applied to the data and, um, Maori practices across it.

Um, are you able to explain to me how it's relevant for governance? I understand the sharing of data with interest groups, it could be really interesting, um, but the data is data. So I'm keen to know your, how this is applied in practice and what your thoughts are about this.

So the NAI framework is the primary way that we provide, um, that we, that te Maori principles are applied to how the data is used. And so we, it's an ethical framework from our perspective, and it's been developed, uh, gifted to stats NZ by, um, by a Maori researcher originally.

Um, and so that is the mechanism, um, by which, um, Maori principles are applied. So, um, I actually don't have the information in front of me about the content of that framework, but it is published. Um, and so we're more than happy to share the details of how that framework works With you. Yeah, yeah. I think, I think in practice, um, if we know that for example, if we know that a research project, um, is going to focus on a particular community, what that framework helps do is um, uh, ask questions about who's on the research team, do you have the right expertise

to be working alongside, um, those communities? Um, and then also I think in terms of the outputs, have you thought about, um, how those out outputs will be presented and um, and analyzed and those sorts of things? So, uh, yeah, it's a set of questions that are really helpful for, um, researchers, um, particularly if they're new to the space as well, and they just needed additional support to work effectively with, um, with different communities.

Okay. And yeah, possible we, could we grab a copy of that framework Absolutely. Point we can provide That, absolutely. Share it. Now, just earlier you mentioned that the infrastructure that this runs is quite freaky. Um, what can you go, are you able to give a similar indication of what that means and, and if there is an opportunity to update that given the technologies that are available now with, without compromise the integrity of the data sets that you hold?

Yeah, I think it's two things. It's the, um, the infrastructure itself, like the IT infrastructure, um, needs an upgrade, but it's also the way that we do the data processing. So, um, you'll appreciate starting from the beginning, the IDI was quite small, it's grown significantly over time, but the kind of architecture and the design of it hasn't changed. And so what happens is three times a year we refresh it and we, um, receive resupply of the data from everybody each time, and we completely reload that data.

And so, um, as it grows and it grows and it grows, and I think it's 1,073 gigabytes and 16 million rows in terms of the total volumes and things that are there now, it becomes increasingly difficult to kind of run that model. And so, yeah, it's not just the technology itself, it's definitely the data processing and the way that that works.

And so we would like to change the way that data is processed, um, so that we could just incrementally update. So that just means that we're only updating new records. Everything that hasn't changed, we don't change. And so that would allow us to update the data much more frequently. Um, and so that's a kind of business case that we are working on. Alright. Yeah. Okay. Um, so, um, a note from, um, the list.

We've got the data in the, the IIPI, um, only a couple, two or three that reach back to the eighties. Is there any intention of, of any way, any way of capturing data prior to the two thousands? So mostly that reflects, um, when these data sets collected by the government agencies that collect them were sort of collected in digital form in the first place.

So prior to that, everything that was in here either didn't exist or it was paper records. And so there isn't really any ability to go back and capture the records that were on paper prior to this. So we are limited by, um, you know, the technology that was in existence for the record keeping Yeah. At That time. So there's no intention of going back and digitizing that historic information. C Certainly not by us, but it may be, uh, it may be something that, um, the individual agencies, um, uh, have in their plans and obviously if they were to do that, then

that would potentially make more data available for the IDI. Yeah, I've, I've got a question just sort of from a personal experience and running how that data set may or may not exist. Um, having, having worked in the criminal justice sector for about 10 years Sure. I I know that, uh, there's a very high incidence of neurodiversity, uh, and other complexities in the people who end up in the court system and the prison system.
